scheme.title: Glossary and definitions
scheme.subtitle: The single-source terminology for the land-transactions portfolio

terms.definition: The termination form for conventional incidents: the separated claim-right is reintegrated into its parent bundle on expiry, surrender, or satisfaction of the obligation it secured. For the corresponding operation on allocated incidents (public-law privileges), see *Extinguishment*; for reserved incidents (PLRs), see *Rescission*. The counterpart of *derivation*.

ladm_mapping.notes: LADM has no operation vocabulary: the ending of an RRR is expressed only as a closed version (VersionedObject endLifespanVersion, ISO 19152-1:2024, 6.3.1). Absorption as reintegration into the parent bundle is not representable as a concept.

terms.source: Beck, A. (2026). Glossary and definitions: the single-source terminology for the land-transactions portfolio.

terms.definition: The conditions a transaction submission must satisfy before it is routed to a decision officer. Applied as automated gates at the pre-submission stage under five principles: specificity (a well-formedness check) and capacity, power, consent, and admissibility (validity checks). Applied in a fail-fast order, automated gating prevents avoidable rework without removing officer discretion. There are five gates; professional capability is a limb of *capacity*.

ladm_mapping.notes: LADM is a content model for recorded objects; transaction gating and validity conditions are out of scope. The nearest hook is that Part 2 administrative sources record the evidence a gate would have examined.

terms.source: Beck, A. (2026). Glossary and definitions: the single-source terminology for the land-transactions portfolio.

terms.definition: In LADM, the object representing the rights package associated with a parcel: it defines what is held and under what conditions. Combined with a spatial unit (geometry), it forms the complete representation of a land object.

ladm_mapping.iso_19152_clause: ISO 19152-1:2024, 3.1.2 (basic administrative unit)
ladm_mapping.notes: Narrower than BAUnit: the glossary definition assumes association with a parcel (one spatial unit), whereas a BAUnit comprises zero or more spatial units and need not be cadastral. Substance otherwise aligned: the rights package against which unique, homogeneous RRRs are recorded.

terms.source: Beck, A. (2026). Glossary and definitions: the single-source terminology for the land-transactions portfolio.

terms.definition: One of the five acceptance criteria (a validity check). Requires that the *resulting state* of a transaction is itself lawful: the right type is within the jurisdiction's recognised set (*numerus clausus*), mandatory rules are satisfied, any plan hierarchy is conformed to, and applicable PLRs are not breached. Distinct from *power* (provenance of authority) and *specificity* (clarity of description), which it relieves of carrying outcome-legality.

ladm_mapping.notes: LADM records what RRRs exist; it does not model the lawfulness conditions of a proposed resulting state. The recognised-set check loosely parallels constraining RRR types to code-list values (ISO 19152-1:2024, Annex C), but no validity concept is defined.

terms.source: Beck, A. (2026). Glossary and definitions: the single-source terminology for the land-transactions portfolio.

terms.definition: Alienation is the **VoI + VoP composite** (derive the incident, then allocate it by transfer), not a single primitive. It is the *necessary minimal pattern* for bringing any derived conventional incident into a holder's hands: a derivation alone leaves the incident within the grantor's holding, so at least one allocating transfer must follow. See *Variation of Incidents*, *Variation of Party*.

ladm_mapping.notes: A composite operation (VoI derivation + allocating VoP). LADM defines no transaction primitives; the deed evidencing an alienation is captured as an administrative source in Part 2 (ISO 19152-2:2025).

terms.source: Beck, A. (2026). Glossary and definitions: the single-source terminology for the land-transactions portfolio.

terms.definition: Public-law authorisations (permits, licences, and time-limited approvals) vested by agencies exercising statutory powers. One of three incident types in the typology used by this paper, alongside *conventional incidents* and *reserved incidents*. In Hohfeldian terms, the issuing authority exercises a *power* to vest a *privilege* in the applicant from the authority's own jural position. The applicant receives something; they do not recover something that was always theirs. Modelled as a VoI derivation with explicit parameterisation of effective period, conditions, and discharge triggers. See *Derivation*, *Variation of Incidents*.

hohfeldian_signature.notes: Class-level signature. The authority exercises a power to vest the privilege; correlative is the general no-right of others to demand the holder refrain. Defaults shown are the class norm (no sub-permitting, no onward dealing); per-type declarations live in the jurisdiction's recognised authorisation set.

ladm_mapping.iso_19152_clause: ISO 19152-5:2025, term 'permit'; cf. ISO 19152-1:2024, 3.1, term 'right' (sub-clause >3.1.10, VERIFY against full text; ISO 19152:2012: 4.1.20)
ladm_mapping.notes: Part 5 introduces 'permit' (permission granted to a party to undertake an otherwise restricted activity). This term refines that: it generalises to all statutory authorisations, supplies the Hohfeldian characterisation (privilege vested from the authority's own jural position, not a lifted restriction), and parameterises lifecycle (effective period, conditions, discharge triggers). Core LADM RRR does not distinguish privilege from claim-right.

terms.source: Beck, A. (2026). Glossary and definitions: the single-source terminology for the land-transactions portfolio.

terms.definition: A change to the *terms* of an existing right relationship (a lease extension, a rent review, a change to an easement's scope) where holder, land, and the right's existence are unchanged. Modelled as a continuing version of the same right relationship under its durable identifier (not as absorption followed by a fresh derivation) so that continuity-dependent facts (commencement date, accrued priority, qualifying periods) survive.

ladm_mapping.iso_19152_clause: ISO 19152-1:2024, 6.3.1 (VersionedObject)
ladm_mapping.notes: Realised in LADM as a new version of the same RRR instance under its identifier — which is exactly the continuing-version semantics this term mandates. LADM supplies the mechanism but does not name the operation or contrast it with terminate-and-re-derive.

terms.source: Beck, A. (2026). Glossary and definitions: the single-source terminology for the land-transactions portfolio.

terms.definition: A defined contract through which one software service requests information or actions from another. An API-first design means authoritative services expose their data and functions through stable, documented, machine-readable interfaces that other systems can reliably consume.

ladm_mapping.notes: Generic IT term; LADM is encoding-independent by design and does not define service interfaces.

terms.source: Beck, A. (2026). Glossary and definitions: the single-source terminology for the land-transactions portfolio.

terms.definition: The second write operation of the ecosystem, alongside transactional variation. The authorised determination of a *derived fact* about a land object (*value*, *use-class*, or *compliance/discharge status*) as at a date, on a stated basis, contestable through the dispute pathway. Assessment ascribes a fact; it does not change entitlement and it does not alter the land object.

ladm_mapping.iso_19152_clause: ISO 19152-4:2025 (valuation information)
ladm_mapping.notes: Part 4 covers the determination of assessed values on a stated basis at a date — the value limb of this term. This term extends that pattern to two further derived facts (use-class and compliance/discharge status) and makes contestability through the dispute pathway part of the concept.

terms.source: Beck, A. (2026). Glossary and definitions: the single-source terminology for the land-transactions portfolio.

terms.definition: The agency with the mandate to define, maintain, publish, and correct a given dataset or service. Other agencies reference the custodian's data rather than maintaining diverging local copies. See also: *once-only principle*, *shadow infrastructure*.

ladm_mapping.notes: LADM identifies parties and sources but defines no custodianship or mandate concept; the register holder appears in Part 2 land registration without a generic custodian term.

terms.source: Beck, A. (2026). Glossary and definitions: the single-source terminology for the land-transactions portfolio.

terms.definition: The recording of two independent timelines on every version of a right or derived fact: *valid time* (when the fact holds in reality which is the basis on which limitation and qualifying periods run) and *transaction time* (when the register recorded it which is the basis for audit and indemnity).

ladm_mapping.iso_19152_clause: ISO 19152-1:2024, 6.3.1 (VersionedObject)
ladm_mapping.notes: Edition II explicitly supports the bi-temporal model: VersionedObject carries system-time lifespans (begin/endLifespanVersion = transaction time) and a second optional set of real-world temporal attributes (begin/endRealWorldLifespanVersion, acceptance = valid time). Terminology differs (lifespan/real-world vs transaction/valid time) but the model is the same; this glossary additionally states what runs on each timeline (limitation/qualifying periods vs audit/indemnity).

terms.source: Beck, A. (2026). Glossary and definitions: the single-source terminology for the land-transactions portfolio.

terms.definition: A standardised graphical notation for representing business processes. Used in this paper to illustrate the permit creation workflow and the agency interactions it depends upon.

ladm_mapping.notes: OMG standard (ISO/IEC 19510), outside the ISO 19152 series.

terms.source: Beck, A. (2026). Glossary and definitions: the single-source terminology for the land-transactions portfolio.

terms.definition: The analysis of property ownership as a collection of separable jural incidents (to possess, use, manage, derive income, transmit, and hold residuary character) rather than a single, indivisible entitlement, following Honoré [-@honore_ownership_1961]. These incidents normally travel together but can be separated, constrained, or reallocated individually. This paper uses *incident* as the preferred general term to capture this compound structure. See *Conventional incidents*, *Incident*, *Hohfeldian analysis*.

ladm_mapping.notes: Not a defined LADM term. LADM's structure is congruent — multiple RRRs recorded against one BAUnit — but LADM does not adopt Honoré's analysis of ownership as separable standard incidents.

terms.source: Beck, A. (2026). Glossary and definitions: the single-source terminology for the land-transactions portfolio.

terms.definition: A register of cadastral units providing stable identifiers, geometry, version history, and topology. The backbone service that all other workflows reference to identify unambiguously "what land" is involved in a transaction or permit.

ladm_mapping.notes: A service concept, not an LADM term. Functionally it operationalises the spatial unit package plus identifier management (Oid, ISO 19152-1:2024, 3.1.10) as a governed register with versioned geometry and topology.

terms.source: Beck, A. (2026). Glossary and definitions: the single-source terminology for the land-transactions portfolio.

terms.definition: The administrative object in LADM representing a parcel of land. It carries stable identifiers, is associated with spatial geometry, and provides the reference point for rights, restrictions, and transactions.

ladm_mapping.iso_19152_clause: ISO 19152-1:2024, 3.1.2 (basic administrative unit)
ladm_mapping.notes: The cadastral specialisation of BAUnit: a registered administrative entity with stable identifiers and associated spatial geometry. BAUnit is wider — it admits zero spatial units (registry without cadastral map) and non-cadastral tenure.

terms.source: Beck, A. (2026). Glossary and definitions: the single-source terminology for the land-transactions portfolio.

terms.definition: "Capability" denotes the professional-competence aspect of *capacity* (the professional registration and certification of supporting parties (e.g., surveyors, conveyancers, certifying engineers)) required for a given transaction type. It is evaluated by the *capacity principle*. See *Capacity principle*.

ladm_mapping.notes: Professional registration/certification of supporting parties is not modelled; LADM party data is largely expected from external registers, which Part 1 places out of scope.

terms.source: Beck, A. (2026). Glossary and definitions: the single-source terminology for the land-transactions portfolio.

terms.definition: One of the five acceptance criteria (a validity check). Requires that parties are legally and professionally capable of granting or receiving rights. Capacity has two limbs: *personal* competence (age, legal competence, inhibitions, sanctions, insolvency freezes and other restrictions on party capacity in the jurisdiction) and *professional* competence (the registration and certification requirements applicable to supporting parties). Professional "capability" is the second limb of this gate, not a separate criterion. See *Capability*.

ladm_mapping.notes: Party capacity conditions (age, competence, inhibitions, insolvency) are not modelled in LADM; party detail is expected from external registers (out of scope per Part 1, Clause 1).

terms.source: Beck, A. (2026). Glossary and definitions: the single-source terminology for the land-transactions portfolio.

terms.definition: In Hohfeldian analysis, the jural position of a holder who is entitled to a specific performance from a correlative duty-bearer. An entitlement enforceable against an identified party. Distinguished from a *privilege*, which merely permits the holder to act without imposing a duty on others. In this paper, conventional private-law incidents (leases, mortgages, easements) create claim-rights in their holders; duty-correlative reserved incidents (planning obligations, restrictive covenants) instantiate the authority's or beneficiary's claim-right with the landowner's duty as its correlative. See *Hohfeldian analysis*, *Privilege*, *No-privilege*.

hohfeldian_signature.notes: Abstract jural position. Divisibility and tradability are type-level properties of specific incident types, not of the position itself.

ladm_mapping.notes: Hohfeldian analytic layer. LADM's 'right' (ISO 19152-1:2024, 3.1, term 'right' (sub-clause >3.1.10, VERIFY against full text; ISO 19152:2012: 4.1.20)) is defined as an entitlement without decomposition into jural positions; this glossary supplies that decomposition on top of the LADM vocabulary.

terms.source: Beck, A. (2026). Glossary and definitions: the single-source terminology for the land-transactions portfolio.

terms.definition: One of the five acceptance criteria (a validity check). Requires that where private rights are affected the right-holder's consent is demonstrated and validated, including verification of any authorised representation.

ladm_mapping.notes: Consent verification is process, not content; out of LADM scope. The instrument evidencing consent would appear only as an administrative source (Part 2).

terms.source: Beck, A. (2026). Glossary and definitions: the single-source terminology for the land-transactions portfolio.

terms.definition: Private-law rights over land created and transferred through conveyancing (e.g., ownership, lease, easement, mortgage). Distinguished from *reserved incidents*, which are public-law constraints imposed by the state.

hohfeldian_signature.notes: Class-level signature. Divisibility and tradability vary by type and are declared per type in the numerus clausus (e.g. lease tradable, easement not).

ladm_mapping.iso_19152_clause: ISO 19152-1:2024, 3.1, term 'right' (sub-clause >3.1.10, VERIFY against full text; ISO 19152:2012: 4.1.20)
ladm_mapping.notes: Narrower than LADM 'right' (formal or informal entitlement): restricted to private-law claim-rights created and transferred through conveyancing, excluding informal/customary tenure and the public-law incidents this typology assigns to the reserved and allocated classes.

terms.source: Beck, A. (2026). Glossary and definitions: the single-source terminology for the land-transactions portfolio.

terms.definition: The legal process of transferring, creating, or varying property rights between parties.

ladm_mapping.notes: The legal process itself is out of scope; LADM records its products (RRRs) and evidence (administrative sources, Part 2).

terms.source: Beck, A. (2026). Glossary and definitions: the single-source terminology for the land-transactions portfolio.

terms.definition: Used in two distinct senses. (1) *Tradability sense*: an onward market transfer of a tradable right after its initial allocation: the subsequent independent trading of freehold, leasehold, mortgage, or strata interests. Every dealing is a transaction, but not every transaction is a dealing: the initial grant of a right is a transaction (alienation) but not a dealing in this sense. (2) *Land registration sense*: a registered act or instrument that alters an entry in the title register, the technical meaning in deeds and title-registration systems. Where context does not disambiguate, "transaction" is the preferred general term. See *Tradable*, *Transaction primitive*, *Alienation*.

ladm_mapping.notes: Not defined in the ISO 19152 series. In the land-registration sense, a dealing corresponds to a registered act evidenced by an administrative source (Part 2); the tradability sense has no LADM hook because market transferability is not modelled.

terms.source: Beck, A. (2026). Glossary and definitions: the single-source terminology for the land-transactions portfolio.

terms.definition: One of the two directions of a *Variation of Incidents*: the establishment of a new jural incident from an authorised source. For conventional incidents, it carves a claim-right (lease, easement, security, strata interest) from a parent bundle, leaving a residual interest. For reserved incidents, it establishes a restriction or responsibility through a statutory or plan-making process. For allocated incidents, it creates a privilege through the exercise of statutory power. The counterpart of *termination*. Conventional derivation is recursive for certain incidents; allocated incidents are not (a permit cannot be sub-permitted).

ladm_mapping.notes: Operation vocabulary absent from LADM: creating an RRR instance is representable only as a new versioned object, not as a typed operation with a source bundle and residual interest.

terms.source: Beck, A. (2026). Glossary and definitions: the single-source terminology for the land-transactions portfolio.

terms.definition: The regulatory function governing what can be built or changed on land, typically administered through a permitting or planning system.

ladm_mapping.notes: The regulatory function sits in the spatial-plan domain addressed by ISO 19152-5:2025 (plan units/blocks, permits), but the function itself is not a defined term.

terms.source: Beck, A. (2026). Glossary and definitions: the single-source terminology for the land-transactions portfolio.

terms.definition: A type-level property of a right: whether it can be held in undivided fractional shares (co-ownership), and therefore whether the fractional (part) form of a *Variation of Party* is available to it. Independent of *tradable*: the shared areas of a real-estate complex are divisible (held in fractional shares) yet not tradable. Declared in the numerus clausus; restrictable per instance by a recorded term.

ladm_mapping.notes: Edition II introduces 'fraction' as the data type for co-ownership shares — the instance-level expression of divisibility. This term adds the type-level rule LADM does not model: whether a right type admits fractional holding at all (declared in the numerus clausus, restrictable per instance).

terms.source: Beck, A. (2026). Glossary and definitions: the single-source terminology for the land-transactions portfolio.

terms.definition: A stable identity for a right or spatial unit that *persists across variations*. Versions of the entity hang from it, so that continuity-dependent facts (commencement date, accrued priority, qualifying periods) survive a variation. A variation produces a new version under the same durable identifier rather than a new entity.

ladm_mapping.iso_19152_clause: ISO 19152-1:2024, 3.1.10 (object identifier); 6.3.1 (VersionedObject)
ladm_mapping.notes: Oid provides generic object identification; VersionedObject hangs versions from an identified object. This term makes the combination a requirement: the identifier must persist across variations precisely so that continuity-dependent facts survive.

terms.source: Beck, A. (2026). Glossary and definitions: the single-source terminology for the land-transactions portfolio.

terms.definition: A right to use another party's land for a specific, defined purpose (e.g., access, drainage, utility corridor). Derived by a VoI and allocated once to the benefiting party by the alienation pattern; being appurtenant, it is referenceable but not tradable onward. Thereafter it runs with the land, moving only when the parcel it benefits is transferred.

hohfeldian_signature.notes: Servient owner bears the correlative duty (to suffer the use / not interfere). Appurtenant: allocated once at grant, thereafter passes with the benefiting parcel — referenceable but not tradable.

ladm_mapping.iso_19152_clause: ISO 19152-1:2024, 3.1, term 'right' (sub-clause >3.1.10, VERIFY against full text; ISO 19152:2012: 4.1.20); appurtenance pattern: ISO 19152-1:2024, 3.1.2, Note 2
ladm_mapping.notes: An LADM right; notably, BAUnit Note 2 describes exactly this glossary's appurtenance behaviour — a BAUnit acting as proxy party to hold an easement as an appurtenance to another BAUnit. This term adds the derivation/allocation analysis (VoI + single allocating VoP, not tradable onward).

terms.source: Beck, A. (2026). Glossary and definitions: the single-source terminology for the land-transactions portfolio.

terms.definition: A right held by a third party that limits (or burdens) a landowners property, limiting the owner's freedom to use  it or transfer its title. While it does not stop the property from being sold, it can diminish its value or impose  ongoing obligations.

hohfeldian_signature.notes: Umbrella term: the third party holds a claim-right against the encumbered estate. Divisibility/tradability vary by the underlying incident type (mortgage tradable; restrictive covenant not).

ladm_mapping.notes: Spans LADM categories rather than mapping to one: an encumbrance may be an LADM right (easement) or restriction (covenant); ISO 19152:2012 modelled mortgage as a special restriction (LA_Mortgage). No umbrella term is defined.

terms.source: Beck, A. (2026). Glossary and definitions: the single-source terminology for the land-transactions portfolio.

terms.definition: An integration pattern in which services publish notifications when their state changes, allowing subscribing systems to react without polling. Used in this strategy to propagate parcel changes, restriction updates, and rights events across the ecosystem.

ladm_mapping.notes: Integration pattern; LADM is encoding- and architecture-independent.

terms.source: Beck, A. (2026). Glossary and definitions: the single-source terminology for the land-transactions portfolio.

terms.definition: The public-law counterpart of *absorption*: the cessation of a privilege (permit, licence, or time-limited approval) on expiry, revocation, or breach of conditions. Unlike absorption, extinguishment does not reintegrate anything into a parent bundle: the privilege simply ceases to exist in the holder's hands, and the holder reverts to their prior position of no-privilege. The authority's power to grant further authorisations is unaffected. See *Absorption*, *Allocated incidents*, *Variation of Incidents*.

ladm_mapping.notes: As with absorption: LADM expresses cessation only as a closed version (endLifespanVersion); it does not distinguish termination forms by incident type.

terms.source: Beck, A. (2026). Glossary and definitions: the single-source terminology for the land-transactions portfolio.

terms.definition: The point in a chain of transactions from which title is traced and on the basis of which subsequent dealings are validated. In a deeds-register tradition, the choice of root determines what the register guarantees; in a title-by-registration system, registration itself provides the root and a state guarantee displaces the need to trace further back. The two register traditions are two projections of one ordered transaction log differentiated by the position of good root of title [@beck_effect_2022]. See *Amendment*, *Bitemporality*, *Priority*.

ladm_mapping.notes: Register-tradition concept belonging to the land-registration domain of ISO 19152-2:2025; not a defined term. The ordered-transaction-log reading of the two traditions is this portfolio's analysis layered on the Part 2 source/version model.

terms.source: Beck, A. (2026). Glossary and definitions: the single-source terminology for the land-transactions portfolio.

terms.definition: The operating condition of a large-scale programme in which frequent, overlapping subdivision, consolidation, and re-plotting of cadastral units must proceed simultaneously with high-volume permitting and the management of complex public-law constraints. The term defines the environment that motivates the ecosystem architecture and the transactional primitives (VoP, VoL, VoI) proposed in this paper. See *VoL*, *VoP*, *VoI*, *PLR*.

ladm_mapping.notes: Operating-condition term specific to this portfolio.

terms.source: Beck, A. (2026). Glossary and definitions: the single-source terminology for the land-transactions portfolio.

terms.definition: A framework for legal analysis, developed by Wesley Hohfeld, that decomposes legal relations into atomic units: claim-rights, privileges (liberties), powers, and immunities. Used in this paper to distinguish a *privilege* (a freedom to act, owed to no other party) from a *claim-right* (an entitlement enforceable against another), and to characterise the legal nature of permits as privilege-allocations from the granting authority's own jural position. See *Claim-right*, *Immunity*, *No-privilege*, *Privilege*.

ladm_mapping.notes: Not adopted by the ISO 19152 series; this framework layers Hohfeld's positions onto the LADM RRR vocabulary, which is one of the portfolio's stated contributions to the LADM track.

terms.source: Beck, A. (2026). Glossary and definitions: the single-source terminology for the land-transactions portfolio.

terms.definition: The systems and processes governing digital identities, authentication (confirming who a party is), authorisation (defining what they may do), delegation and representation relationships, and the audit trail of actions taken under each identity. In this strategy, IAM is the recommended implementation vehicle for the *Party and Representation service*, covering representation chains, power-of-attorney delegation, company signatory verification, and audited access to transaction services.

ladm_mapping.notes: Party/identity infrastructure is explicitly outside LADM scope (Part 1, Clause 1 excludes external party databases); IAM is this strategy's implementation vehicle for that external dependency.

terms.source: Beck, A. (2026). Glossary and definitions: the single-source terminology for the land-transactions portfolio.

terms.definition: A stable, unique reference code assigned to an entity (party, parcel, restriction, permit) that persists across system updates and enables consistent, unambiguous cross-agency referencing.

ladm_mapping.iso_19152_clause: ISO 19152-1:2024, 3.1.10 (object identifier)
ladm_mapping.notes: Matches Oid: a generic, stable identifier supporting object identification across parties, spatial units and RRRs. BAUnit Note 3 likewise expects a unique identifier on registration.

terms.source: Beck, A. (2026). Glossary and definitions: the single-source terminology for the land-transactions portfolio.

terms.definition: In Hohfeldian analysis, the jural position that protects its holder from another party's power. The correlative of a *disability* (the other party's incapacity to alter that holder's jural position). Distinct from a *privilege* (freedom from a duty) and a *claim-right* (entitlement against a duty-bearer). Immunities arise in land administration contexts where legislative protections prevent an authority from unilaterally altering a registered right without due process. See *Hohfeldian analysis*, *Claim-right*, *Privilege*.

hohfeldian_signature.notes: Second-order position: protects the holder from another party's power; that party's incapacity (disability) is the correlative.

ladm_mapping.notes: Hohfeldian analytic layer; no LADM counterpart. Relevant in land administration where legislative protections disable unilateral alteration of registered rights, which LADM records but does not characterise.

terms.source: Beck, A. (2026). Glossary and definitions: the single-source terminology for the land-transactions portfolio.

terms.definition: The preferred general term in this paper for any jural position that attaches to, or is allocated over, land: adopted in preference to "right" to reflect the compound and separable structure of what land ownership involves. Three types are distinguished: *conventional incidents* (private-law claim-rights created through conveyancing), *reserved incidents* (public-law restrictions and responsibilities imposed by statute or plan-making), and *allocated incidents* (public-law privileges vested by statutory authority). The term follows Honoré's [-@honore_ownership_1961] analysis of ownership as a bundle of separable standard incidents. See *Bundle of rights*, *Conventional incidents*, *Reserved incidents*, *Allocated incidents*.

hohfeldian_signature.notes: Umbrella term — carries no signature of its own; each incident type (conventional, reserved, allocated) bears its own signature in the numerus clausus.

ladm_mapping.iso_19152_clause: ISO 19152-1:2024, 3.1.2, Note 6 (RRR); ISO 19152-1:2024, 3.1, term 'right' (sub-clause >3.1.10, VERIFY against full text; ISO 19152:2012: 4.1.20)
ladm_mapping.notes: Plays the role of LADM's collective 'RRR' (rights, restrictions, responsibilities) but reframes it on Honoré/Hohfeld and adds a third public-law class (allocated incidents (privileges)) that the RRR triple does not distinguish from rights.

terms.source: Beck, A. (2026). Glossary and definitions: the single-source terminology for the land-transactions portfolio.

terms.definition: ISO 19152; an international standard providing a conceptual model and shared vocabulary for land administration systems. Supplies the Party--Right--Land framework underpinning the VoP/VoL/VoI transaction primitives used in this strategy.

ladm_mapping.iso_19152_clause: ISO 19152 series; Part 1: ISO 19152-1:2024
ladm_mapping.notes: The glossary entry describes the standard itself. Note the 2012 single-document edition is superseded by the multi-part series (Parts 1-5 published 2024-2025).

terms.source: Beck, A. (2026). Glossary and definitions: the single-source terminology for the land-transactions portfolio.

terms.definition: In LADM  the composite entity comprising an *administrative unit* (the rights package) and its associated *spatial unit* (geometry/extent). The term emphasises the spatial reference used when identifying what land is involved in a transaction or permit. *Property object* refers to the same entity emphasised as the first-class holder of a rights bundle from which incidents are derived or into which they are absorbed. See *Administrative unit*, *Spatial unit*, *Property object*.

ladm_mapping.iso_19152_clause: ISO 19152-1:2024, 3.1.2 (basic administrative unit) + ISO 19152-1:2024, 3.1, term 'spatial unit' (sub-clause >3.1.10, VERIFY against full text; ISO 19152:2012: 4.1.23)
ladm_mapping.notes: Composite of two LADM terms — the administrative unit (rights package) and its spatial unit (geometry) — for which LADM defines no single term. Distinct from LADM 'land' (3.1.8), which is the generic spatial extent, not a unitised object.

terms.source: Beck, A. (2026). Glossary and definitions: the single-source terminology for the land-transactions portfolio.

terms.definition: A shared service that mints stable, persistent cross-agency identifiers for land objects and resolves any identifier to the authoritative service that owns it. It does not consolidate geometry; it provides a single resolution point so that rights and permits can unambiguously reference "what land" regardless of which custodian holds the authoritative geometry. New identifiers are published only after the relevant authority service has validated and committed the corresponding spatial change. See *Cadastral index*, *Identifier*, *Once-only principle*.

ladm_mapping.notes: Service concept: mints and resolves cross-agency identifiers (cf. Oid, 3.1.10) without consolidating geometry. LADM defines the identifier, not the resolution infrastructure.

terms.source: Beck, A. (2026). Glossary and definitions: the single-source terminology for the land-transactions portfolio.

terms.definition: The set of properties on a jural incident that govern when it activates and when it terminates: an effective period, the conditions under which it activates, and the triggers (expiry, surrender, satisfaction of an obligation, a staged-release milestone, or statutory rescission) that cause termination. Treating lifecycle as a first-class property of each incident type means that leases, easements, securities, permits, and PLR zones share the same structural shape with different parameters, rather than requiring separate instrument logic for each. See *Termination*, *Amendment*, *VoI*.

ladm_mapping.iso_19152_clause: ISO 19152-1:2024, 6.3.1 (VersionedObject)
ladm_mapping.notes: VersionedObject gives every instance lifespans (system and real-world time). This term adds what LADM does not model: activation conditions and typed termination triggers (expiry, surrender, satisfaction, milestone, rescission) as first-class properties of each incident type.

terms.source: Beck, A. (2026). Glossary and definitions: the single-source terminology for the land-transactions portfolio.

terms.definition: The legal authority conferred on an agency to perform a specific public function. In this strategy, agencies improve services within their existing mandate; no transfer of mandates between agencies is required.

ladm_mapping.notes: Agency mandates are institutional context, not LADM content.

terms.source: Beck, A. (2026). Glossary and definitions: the single-source terminology for the land-transactions portfolio.

terms.definition: In Hohfeldian analysis, the absence of a privilege: the jural position of a party that lacks the freedom to perform a given act. In this paper, no-privilege is the precise characterisation of a landowner's position with respect to a restricted activity under a public-law constraint. The landowner does not hold a duty-not-to-develop (which would imply a claim-right in another party that could be released), but rather lacks the privilege to act in the first place. A permit does not lift a restriction; the issuing authority vests a privilege from its own jural position. See *Privilege*, *Hohfeldian analysis*, *Allocated incidents*, *Reserved incidents*.

hohfeldian_signature.notes: Deliberate departure from the strict first-order table, where absence of privilege to do A is equivalent to a duty not to do A (correlative: another's claim-right). This paper uses no-privilege precisely where there is no releasable private claim-right: the development right is reserved to the authority, which holds the corresponding privilege/power in its own jural position. A permit supplies a privilege; it does not release a duty.

ladm_mapping.notes: Hohfeldian analytic layer. LADM would record the underlying situation as a restriction; the no-privilege characterisation (and its consequence: a permit supplies a privilege rather than lifting a restriction) is this portfolio's analysis.

terms.source: Beck, A. (2026). Glossary and definitions: the single-source terminology for the land-transactions portfolio.

terms.definition: Latin for "closed number." The principle that only a defined set of right types can be carved from a bundle and vested to third parties; parties cannot create novel incidents with the character of property by agreement alone. In this paper, the numerus clausus is the typed registry of derivable incident types for a jurisdiction, each carrying a Hohfeldian signature (jural position, correlative, divisibility, tradability). The *admissibility* gate checks that a proposed transaction produces an incident within this recognised set. See *Admissibility principle*, *Rights Ontology service*, *VoI*.

ladm_mapping.notes: Nearest LADM device is the code lists for RRR types (ISO 19152-1:2024, Annex C), which enumerate recognised types without Hohfeldian signatures, power requirements or admissibility constraints. The typed registry described here is richer than a code list.

terms.source: Beck, A. (2026). Glossary and definitions: the single-source terminology for the land-transactions portfolio.

terms.definition: The design principle that authoritative information should be recorded and maintained once by the responsible custodian and reused by other agencies through governed services, rather than re-entered or re-created in local copies that may diverge. See also: *authoritative custodian*, *shadow infrastructure*.

ladm_mapping.notes: Digital-government design principle (EU once-only/EIF lineage), not an ISO 19152 term, though LADM's single-source RRR recording is compatible with it.

terms.source: Beck, A. (2026). Glossary and definitions: the single-source terminology for the land-transactions portfolio.

terms.definition: In LADM, an entity (e.g., natural person, legal person, or organisation) that can hold registerable rights or act in land transactions. Parties also possess *powers* that determine what transactions they can lawfully initiate or consent to.

ladm_mapping.iso_19152_clause: ISO 19152-1:2024, 3.1, term 'party' (sub-clause >3.1.10, VERIFY against full text; ISO 19152:2012: 4.1.13); group party: ISO 19152-1:2024, 3.1.7
ladm_mapping.notes: Matches LADM party (person or organisation that can hold RRRs and act in transactions); LADM adds group party (3.1.7) and party member structure. The note that parties possess powers determining what they can initiate is this glossary's Hohfeldian addition.

terms.source: Beck, A. (2026). Glossary and definitions: the single-source terminology for the land-transactions portfolio.

terms.definition: A collection of documented, reusable compositions of transaction primitives (VoP, VoL, VoI) covering recurring complex scenarios (e.g., real estate complexes, utility corridors, stacked rights in multi-use buildings). Patterns allow complex workflows to be assembled from known building blocks without expanding the underlying transaction surface.

ladm_mapping.notes: Documentation device of this portfolio; no LADM counterpart, though the patterns compose into structures LADM can record (e.g. condominium per BAUnit 3.1.2, Example 2).

terms.source: Beck, A. (2026). Glossary and definitions: the single-source terminology for the land-transactions portfolio.

terms.definition: A time-bound authorisation issued by a public authority, conferring on the holder a lawful permission to undertake an otherwise restricted activity on specified land, subject to defined conditions and an effective period.

hohfeldian_signature.notes: Privilege vested by the authority exercising a statutory power from its own jural position. Personal to the holder by default; ends by extinguishment, not absorption.

ladm_mapping.iso_19152_clause: ISO 19152-5:2025, term 'permit'
ladm_mapping.notes: Part 5 defines permit as something granted to a party giving permission to undertake an otherwise restricted activity. This term refines it: issued by a public authority, time-bound, condition-bearing, with an effective period. Aligning the LADM Part 5 concept with the allocated-incident lifecycle model.

terms.source: Beck, A. (2026). Glossary and definitions: the single-source terminology for the land-transactions portfolio.

terms.definition: The layered structure of planning and land regulation instruments (national policy, regional plan, local plan, site-level conditions) in which lower-tier instruments must conform with those above them. The admissibility gate applies at two levels: it checks that a proposed transaction conforms with applicable PLR constraints *and* that any plan-making instrument is consistent with the tier above it. See *Admissibility principle*, *PLR*, *Reserved incidents*.

ladm_mapping.iso_19152_clause: ISO 19152-5:2025 (plan unit / plan block / plan group)
ladm_mapping.notes: Part 5 models the structural hierarchy of spatial plans. This term adds the normative rule the structure exists to enforce (lower-tier instruments must conform with those above) and routes that rule through the admissibility gate.

terms.source: Beck, A. (2026). Glossary and definitions: the single-source terminology for the land-transactions portfolio.

terms.definition: A restriction, constraint, or responsibility imposed on land or its use by legislation, regulation, or planning instrument (e.g., planning zones, heritage designations, environmental buffers, safety exclusion zones). Treated in this strategy as first-class, spatially queryable objects with defined authority, versioning, and applicability conditions. See also: *reserved incidents*.

hohfeldian_signature.notes: Signature shown is for duty-correlative PLRs (claim-right pole held by the authority or beneficiary). Disability-type PLRs are different: the landowner sits in no-privilege and the authority holds the reserved privilege/power — there is no releasable duty/claim-right pair.

ladm_mapping.iso_19152_clause: ISO 19152-1:2024, 3.1, term 'restriction' (sub-clause >3.1.10, VERIFY against full text; ISO 19152:2012: 4.1.19); spatial plans: ISO 19152-5:2025
ladm_mapping.notes: The public-law subset of LADM restriction (and responsibility), made first-class: defined authority, versioning, spatial queryability and applicability conditions. Part 5 supplies the spatial-plan instruments that create many PLRs; the requirement that PLRs be spatially queryable objects subject to VoL is this portfolio's addition.

terms.source: Beck, A. (2026). Glossary and definitions: the single-source terminology for the land-transactions portfolio.

terms.definition: A legal instrument by which a principal authorises a named attorney to act on their behalf for defined purposes. In this strategy, a PoA is a representation instrument: the attorney holds no independent power, so the power and capacity acceptance checks run against the *principal*. The *Party and Representation service* must verify that the PoA is valid, current, and covers the proposed transaction type; an enduring PoA additionally requires confirmation that capacity was present at execution. See *Representation*, *Capacity principle*, *Power principle*.

ladm_mapping.notes: Representation instruments are not modelled; LADM party data (and hence agency relations) is expected from external registers, out of scope per Part 1, Clause 1.

terms.source: Beck, A. (2026). Glossary and definitions: the single-source terminology for the land-transactions portfolio.

terms.definition: The legal capacity to effect a specific change in a Party--Right--Land relationship. Private-law powers derive from rights held (a party can only grant what it has); public-law powers derive from legislative or regulatory authority. Verification of power is a prerequisite for any valid transaction.

hohfeldian_signature.notes: Second-order position: the capacity to change a jural relation. 'Liability' is the technical Hohfeldian correlative (susceptibility to having one's position changed), not culpability.

ladm_mapping.notes: Hohfeldian analytic layer. LADM does not define legal power; the glossary's claim that verification of power is prerequisite to any valid transaction is process, outside the LADM content model.

terms.source: Beck, A. (2026). Glossary and definitions: the single-source terminology for the land-transactions portfolio.

terms.definition: One of the five acceptance criteria (a validity check). Requires that every transaction be supported by the granter's legal power, whether derived from rights held or from legislative empowerment.

ladm_mapping.notes: Validity gating is out of LADM scope; the granter's power would be evidenced only indirectly via administrative sources (Part 2).

terms.source: Beck, A. (2026). Glossary and definitions: the single-source terminology for the land-transactions portfolio.

terms.definition: A right that attaches to land rather than to a person, and runs with the land when it is transferred. A praedial relationship means that one parcel holds rights in another parcel, with those rights passing automatically to whoever owns the benefiting parcel.

hohfeldian_signature.notes: Held by whoever owns the benefiting parcel; the servient parcel's owner bears the duty. Not independently tradable — passes automatically (appurtenant) with the benefiting parcel.

ladm_mapping.iso_19152_clause: ISO 19152-1:2024, 3.1, term 'right' (sub-clause >3.1.10, VERIFY against full text; ISO 19152:2012: 4.1.20); appurtenance: ISO 19152-1:2024, 3.1.2, Note 2
ladm_mapping.notes: An LADM right with the run-with-the-land behaviour that BAUnit Note 2 captures structurally: a BAUnit may act as proxy party to hold a right (e.g. easement) as an appurtenance to another BAUnit — i.e. parcel-holds-right-in-parcel, exactly this term's praedial relationship.

terms.source: Beck, A. (2026). Glossary and definitions: the single-source terminology for the land-transactions portfolio.

terms.definition: The ranking between competing rights over the same land object (typically securities). A property of the *relationship* between rights, not of any single right: the default order derives from valid time (first in time) and is overridden by explicit subordination, postponement, or tacking transactions. A change of priority is itself a first-class, validated transaction.

ladm_mapping.notes: Not a defined term. ISO 19152:2012 carried ranking only as an attribute of LA_Mortgage; treating priority as a first-class relationship between rights, with subordination/postponement/tacking as validated transactions, is this portfolio's extension.

terms.source: Beck, A. (2026). Glossary and definitions: the single-source terminology for the land-transactions portfolio.

terms.definition: In Hohfeldian analysis, a freedom to perform an act without owing a duty to any other party to refrain from it. Distinguished from a *claim-right*. In this paper, issuing a permit is characterised as the authority allocating a privilege from its own jural position to the applicant. It is not restoring a pre-existing entitlement to someone from whom it was withheld.

hohfeldian_signature.notes: Abstract jural position: freedom to act owed to no one. Correlative is others' no-right (no claim that the holder refrain). Opposite is duty.

ladm_mapping.notes: Hohfeldian analytic layer; no LADM counterpart. Its closest application surface is Part 5's permit, which this typology characterises as a privilege-allocation.

terms.source: Beck, A. (2026). Glossary and definitions: the single-source terminology for the land-transactions portfolio.

terms.definition: A legal construct comprising an administrative unit (the rights package) and its associated spatial unit (geometry). This is considered specifically as the first-class holder of a rights bundle. Distinguished from *land object* (which emphasises the spatial reference) in contexts where rights are being derived from or absorbed back into the bundle: a VoI absorption takes a separated right back into the property object's bundle rather than simply referencing the land's spatial extent.

ladm_mapping.iso_19152_clause: ISO 19152-1:2024, 3.1.2 (basic administrative unit) + ISO 19152-1:2024, 3.1, term 'spatial unit' (sub-clause >3.1.10, VERIFY against full text; ISO 19152:2012: 4.1.23)
ladm_mapping.notes: Same composite as land-object (no single LADM term), emphasised as the first-class holder of the rights bundle from which incidents are derived and into which they are absorbed. BAUnit Note 2's proxy-party pattern shows LADM already treating the unit as capable of holding rights.

terms.source: Beck, A. (2026). Glossary and definitions: the single-source terminology for the land-transactions portfolio.

terms.definition: A multi-unit development (block of flats, mixed-use compound) in which individual units are separately owned and common areas are held collectively by unit owners. Modelled in this strategy as a documented composition of VoL, VoI and VoP transactions (see Appendix 1 of the conceptual foundation).

ladm_mapping.notes: Not a defined term, but directly recognised in examples: BAUnit 3.1.2, Example 2 cites a condominium unit comprising multiple spatial units; ISO 19152:2012 modelled legal space building units. The VoL/VoI/VoP composition of the complex is this portfolio's contribution.

terms.source: Beck, A. (2026). Glossary and definitions: the single-source terminology for the land-transactions portfolio.

terms.definition: The property, required of every first-class object (party, parcel, right, PLR, permit), of carrying a durable or at least trusted, authoritative, versioned identifier so that other services can depend on it. The foundation for the once-only principle.

ladm_mapping.notes: Property of objects required by this framework; LADM gestures at it via Oid (3.1.10) and BAUnit Note 3 (unique identifier on registration) but defines no referenceability requirement.

terms.source: Beck, A. (2026). Glossary and definitions: the single-source terminology for the land-transactions portfolio.

terms.definition: The legally recognised authority of one party (agent, attorney, company signatory) to act on behalf of another in land transactions. The ecosystem must model and verify representation consistently so that "who can act for whom?" can be answered auditably at every submission.

ladm_mapping.notes: Group party (3.1.7) and party-member structures cover collective holding, not agency. Acting-on-behalf-of (attorneys, signatories) is unmodelled; party detail is external to LADM scope.

terms.source: Beck, A. (2026). Glossary and definitions: the single-source terminology for the land-transactions portfolio.

terms.definition: The termination form for reserved incidents: a PLR or other public-law restriction is brought to an end through the same statutory or regulatory process that created it. The counterpart of its derivation. Distinguished from *extinguishment* (which applies to allocated incidents/privileges) and *absorption* (which applies to conventional incidents). See *Termination*, *Variation of Incidents*.

ladm_mapping.notes: As with absorption/extinguishment: LADM has no termination-form vocabulary; a rescinded PLR is just a closed version of a restriction.

terms.source: Beck, A. (2026). Glossary and definitions: the single-source terminology for the land-transactions portfolio.

terms.definition: Public-law constraints imposed by the state that limit what the holder of conventional property rights may do with land (e.g., planning controls, environmental restrictions). Permits may enable activities that would otherwise be restricted under reserved incidents. See also: *PLR*.

hohfeldian_signature.notes: Signature shown for the duty-correlative form, taken from the holder's side: the recorded incident instantiates the authority's or beneficiary's claim-right with the landowner's duty as correlative. Disability-type reserved incidents instead leave the landowner in no-privilege (see *No-privilege*).

ladm_mapping.iso_19152_clause: ISO 19152-1:2024, 3.1, term 'restriction' (sub-clause >3.1.10, VERIFY against full text; ISO 19152:2012: 4.1.19) and ISO 19152-1:2024, 3.1, term 'responsibility' (sub-clause >3.1.10, VERIFY against full text; ISO 19152:2012: 4.1.18)
ladm_mapping.notes: The public-law subset of LADM restrictions and responsibilities — those imposed by statute or plan-making rather than private instrument. LADM does not partition RRRs by legal source; this typology does, because derivation and termination pathways differ by source.

terms.source: Beck, A. (2026). Glossary and definitions: the single-source terminology for the land-transactions portfolio.

terms.definition: A positive duty (the holder must perform rather than merely refrain) that may run with land or party (e.g., a development contribution, an affordable-housing condition, a maintenance obligation). In Hohfeldian terms, a responsibility is the landowners' duty that results from a claim-right held by a third party; it differs from a *restriction* only in the direction of the obligation (positive vs negative). A responsibility may arise as a component of a reserved incident (imposed by statutory or plan-making process) or a conventional incident (a positive covenant in a private instrument). Its discharge status is determined by *assessment* and may be enforced as a charge on default. See *Restriction*, *Reserved incidents*, *Hohfeldian analysis*.

hohfeldian_signature.notes: Positive duty (must perform), taken from the burdened party's side; the claim-right is held by a third party. Differs from restriction only in the direction of the obligation.

ladm_mapping.iso_19152_clause: ISO 19152-1:2024, 3.1, term 'responsibility' (sub-clause >3.1.10, VERIFY against full text; ISO 19152:2012: 4.1.18)
ladm_mapping.notes: LADM: formal or informal obligation to do something. This term keeps that core and adds the Hohfeldian reading (duty-pole with a third-party claim-right pole), the symmetry with restriction (direction of obligation only), and discharge determination via assessment.

terms.source: Beck, A. (2026). Glossary and definitions: the single-source terminology for the land-transactions portfolio.

terms.definition: A negative duty (the holder must refrain) imposed on land or its use. In Hohfeldian terms a restriction is the landowners' duty that results from a claim-right held by a third party; it differs from a *responsibility* only in the direction of the obligation (negative vs positive). A duty-correlative restriction (a restrictive covenant, a planning obligation owed to an identified body) is released when that holder waives or is satisfied; a disability-type restriction (a development right reserved to the authority) leaves the landowner in a position of *no-privilege* that a permit does not lift but a privilege-allocation supplies. See *Responsibility*, *Reserved incidents*, *No-privilege*.

hohfeldian_signature.notes: Negative duty (must refrain), taken from the burdened party's side, for the duty-correlative form. Disability-type restrictions are not duties at all: the landowner is in no-privilege and the authority holds the reserved position.

ladm_mapping.iso_19152_clause: ISO 19152-1:2024, 3.1, term 'restriction' (sub-clause >3.1.10, VERIFY against full text; ISO 19152:2012: 4.1.19)
ladm_mapping.notes: LADM: formal or informal obligation to refrain. This term keeps the duty-correlative core but flags an edge LADM's obligation-framing obscures: disability-type 'restrictions' (development rights reserved to the authority) are not duties at all — the landowner is in no-privilege, which is why a permit allocates a privilege rather than releasing anything.

terms.source: Beck, A. (2026). Glossary and definitions: the single-source terminology for the land-transactions portfolio.

terms.definition: A proposed platform service holding the typed *numerus clausus* for a jurisdiction: the schema of permissible incident types, each with its Hohfeldian signature, the power requirements for each transaction type, capacity rules, and admissibility constraints. Separating the rules governing what rights *may* exist from the register recording what rights *do* exist allows the rule set to be versioned independently as legal regimes evolve. See *Numerus clausus*, *Admissibility principle*, *VoI*.

ladm_mapping.notes: Service concept. Its content corresponds to a much-enriched version of LADM's RRR-type code lists (Annex C): typed incidents with Hohfeldian signatures, power requirements, capacity rules and admissibility constraints, versioned independently of the register.

terms.source: Beck, A. (2026). Glossary and definitions: the single-source terminology for the land-transactions portfolio.

terms.definition: A right granted over land to secure a financial obligation (e.g., a mortgage or charge). Derived by a VoI; it is discharged and absorbed back into the parent cadastral unit when the underlying obligation is met.

hohfeldian_signature.notes: Creditor's claim-right carved from the parent bundle to secure performance of an obligation; discharged and absorbed on satisfaction. Tradable on the conveyancing market; priority between competing securities is a property of the relationship between rights, not of the right.

ladm_mapping.iso_19152_clause: ISO 19152:2012 (LA_Mortgage, modelled as a special restriction of the ownership right); cf. ISO 19152-1:2024, 3.1, term 'restriction' (sub-clause >3.1.10, VERIFY against full text; ISO 19152:2012: 4.1.19)
ladm_mapping.notes: Deliberate divergence: this framework models a security as a conventional incident — the creditor's claim-right carved from the parent bundle by VoI and absorbed on discharge — whereas ISO 19152:2012 modelled mortgage as a restriction on the owner's right. The two views attach the object to different poles of the relation; the divergence is intentional and should be surfaced in the SWG mapping.

terms.source: Beck, A. (2026). Glossary and definitions: the single-source terminology for the land-transactions portfolio.

terms.definition: Workaround systems or local data copies created by downstream agencies when authoritative services do not provide the identifier stability, versioning, or quality those agencies need. Shadow infrastructures diverge from the authoritative source over time and undermine the once-only principle.

ladm_mapping.notes: Failure-mode concept of this strategy; no LADM counterpart.

terms.source: Beck, A. (2026). Glossary and definitions: the single-source terminology for the land-transactions portfolio.

terms.definition: In LADM, the geometric extent component of a land object. Combined with an administrative unit (the rights package), it fully represents what practitioners informally call a "parcel."

ladm_mapping.iso_19152_clause: ISO 19152-1:2024, 3.1, term 'spatial unit' (sub-clause >3.1.10, VERIFY against full text; ISO 19152:2012: 4.1.23)
ladm_mapping.notes: Matches LADM spatial unit: the geometric extent component which, with the administrative unit, represents the parcel.

terms.source: Beck, A. (2026). Glossary and definitions: the single-source terminology for the land-transactions portfolio.

terms.definition: One of the five acceptance criteria, and the only *well-formedness* check (the others test validity). Requires that a transaction unambiguously identifies: the *subject* (the Party--Right--Land state being changed), the *transformation* (VoP, VoL, or VoI), and the *variables* (the parameters needed to evaluate and enact the change).

ladm_mapping.notes: Well-formedness gating is out of LADM scope, though the subject/transformation/variables triple is articulated against the LADM Party-Right-Land frame.

terms.source: Beck, A. (2026). Glossary and definitions: the single-source terminology for the land-transactions portfolio.

terms.definition: A form of property ownership in multi-storey or multi-unit buildings in which individual units are separately owned and common areas are held collectively by unit owners. Modelled in this strategy as a VoI derivation from the principal cadastral unit.

hohfeldian_signature.notes: The unit estate trades on the conveyancing market. The accompanying fractional share in common property is divisible (held in undivided shares) but not separately tradable — it passes with the unit.

ladm_mapping.iso_19152_clause: ISO 19152-1:2024, 3.1, term 'right' (sub-clause >3.1.10, VERIFY against full text; ISO 19152:2012: 4.1.20); condominium example: ISO 19152-1:2024, 3.1.2, Example 2
ladm_mapping.notes: An ownership form LADM recognises by example (a condominium unit comprising an apartment and a garage as one BAUnit) and via legal space building units in ISO 19152:2012. This term adds the derivation analysis: a VoI derivation from the principal cadastral unit.

terms.source: Beck, A. (2026). Glossary and definitions: the single-source terminology for the land-transactions portfolio.

terms.definition: The ending direction of a *Variation of Incidents*: the operation that closes an existing jural incident. Termination takes a form matched to the incident type. *Absorption* applies to conventional incidents: the separated claim-right is reintegrated into its parent bundle (on expiry, surrender, or satisfaction of the obligation it secured). *Extinguishment* applies to allocated incidents (public-law privileges): the privilege simply ceases to exist in the holder's hands (on expiry, revocation, or breach of conditions), with nothing returning to any parent holding. *Rescission* applies to reserved incidents: a PLR is brought to an end through the same statutory or regulatory process that created it. All three forms are first-class operations; recording a derivation without a termination pathway leaves dangling encumbrances. See *Absorption*, *Extinguishment*, *Derivation*, *Variation of Incidents*.

ladm_mapping.notes: LADM expresses all endings identically as a closed version (endLifespanVersion, 6.3.1); the three-form typology (absorption/extinguishment/rescission matched to incident type) is this portfolio's operation-level extension.

terms.source: Beck, A. (2026). Glossary and definitions: the single-source terminology for the land-transactions portfolio.

terms.definition: A type-level property of a derived right: whether it can undergo *subsequent, independent* transfers on a market *after* its initial allocation. The conveyancing market transacts tradable rights (freehold, leasehold, strata, mortgage), which demands persistent, chain-traceable, priority-bearing identifiers. Tradability governs *onward* dealing only. Every derived right is allocated once by the alienation pattern regardless of tradability; a non-tradable right (an easement) is allocated at grant and thereafter passes appurtenant to its parent. Independent of *divisible*; tradability implies referenceability, but not the reverse. Restrictable per instance by a recorded term (e.g., a non-assignability covenant).

ladm_mapping.notes: Type-level market-transferability rule; LADM records transfers' outcomes but does not model whether a right type admits onward dealing. Contrast 'divisible', whose instance-level expression (fraction, 3.1.5) Edition II does model.

terms.source: Beck, A. (2026). Glossary and definitions: the single-source terminology for the land-transactions portfolio.

terms.definition: One of the three core, reusable state-change patterns (VoP, VoL, VoI) from which land administration workflows are composed. Each is a variation representing a subdivision or consolidation along one axis of the party--right--land triple. Treating these as primitives keeps the transaction service surface small and stable, while allowing complex scenarios to be built as documented compositions.

ladm_mapping.notes: LADM is deliberately a static content model; it has no transaction layer. The VoP/VoL/VoI primitives are this portfolio's operational algebra over the LADM Party-Right-Land triple, with Part 2 administrative sources as the evidence trail.

terms.source: Beck, A. (2026). Glossary and definitions: the single-source terminology for the land-transactions portfolio.

terms.definition: The primitive that creates, varies, or terminates any jural incident (conventional, reserved, or allocated) associated with a land object. Its two directions are *derivation* (establishing a new incident from an authorised source) and *termination* (closing an existing incident). Termination form is matched to incident type: *absorption* for conventional incidents, *extinguishment* for allocated incidents, *rescission* for reserved incidents. The recognised set covers the private-law *numerus clausus*, the jurisdiction's recognised PLR types, and its recognised statutory authorisation types. Conventional derivation is recursive; allocated incidents are not. For conventional incidents, VoI derivation is always followed by an allocating VoP (the *alienation* pattern); for allocated incidents, derivation and transfer are a single act. VoI also separates jural content changes (its domain) from spatial extent changes (VoL's domain).

ladm_mapping.notes: No LADM counterpart: creates/varies/terminates RRR instances that LADM can only show as version chains. The recognised-set constraint corresponds loosely to RRR-type code lists (Annex C).

terms.source: Beck, A. (2026). Glossary and definitions: the single-source terminology for the land-transactions portfolio.

terms.definition: A generic transaction pattern that changes *what land* a right bundle describes through subdivision or consolidation of spatial units. Because VoL is generic, it applies to any spatially-defined right object: cadastral parcels, planning zones, environmental buffers, heritage areas, and other public-law restriction (PLR) objects. Any alteration to a PLR's spatial extent (redrawing a zone boundary, extending a buffer, splitting a heritage area) is a VoL operation subject to the same acceptance criteria as a cadastral subdivision. **A boundary determination that fixes, adjusts, or re-defines authoritative geometry is a VoL**. VoL is the operational workhorse of high-velocity land change; it requires geometry integrity checks (no gaps or overlaps), recognised survey inputs, and versioned identifier management so that permits and rights always reference the correct spatial state.

ladm_mapping.notes: No LADM counterpart as an operation. Its effects are representable in the spatial unit package (subdivision/consolidation of spatial units, versioned geometry), and Edition II's versioned sources strengthen the audit trail, but LADM names no spatial-variation operation.

terms.source: Beck, A. (2026). Glossary and definitions: the single-source terminology for the land-transactions portfolio.

terms.definition: The primitive that varies who holds a right/incident. Where the right is *divisible*, the holding is a fraction and a VoP may subdivide it (a part transfer creating co-ownership) or consolidate it (re-uniting co-owners' shares); for an indivisible right a VoP transfers the whole or nothing. The whole-or-part transfer of a holding from one party to another is its familiar pattern, the *transfer of party*. A VoP can act on a tradable right as an independent object; in addition, every derived right receives at least one allocating VoP at grant (the alienation pattern), even when it is not tradable thereafter.

ladm_mapping.notes: No LADM counterpart as an operation. Its fractional form lands on Edition II's new 'fraction' type (3.1.5) for co-ownership shares; the transfer itself is visible in LADM only as successive versions of the party-RRR association.

terms.source: Beck, A. (2026). Glossary and definitions: the single-source terminology for the land-transactions portfolio.
